g.data-narration: Welcome to Signal Concepts! This module covers fundamental signal processing ideas essential for DSP and communication systems.
A signal is a function that conveys information about a phenomenon. In engineering, signals represent physical quantities varying with time or space.
g.text: A signal is a function that conveys information about a phenomenon or quantity.
Mathematically: x(t) for continuous-time, x[n] for discrete-time
Example: audio waves, images, data packets, sensor readings

g.data-narration: Signals are classified into several categories based on their properties: continuous vs discrete time, periodic vs aperiodic, and more.
Continuous-time signals are defined for every value of time, like sine waves. Discrete-time signals are defined only at specific instants.
A periodic signal repeats its pattern at regular intervals defined by fundamental period T. Aperiodic signals show no repetition.
Even signals are symmetric about the y-axis: x(-t) equals x(t). Odd signals are symmetric about the origin: x(-t) equals negative x(t).
Any signal can be decomposed into even and odd components using formulas: xe(t) equals x(t) plus x(-t) over 2, and xo(t) equals x(t) minus x(-t) over 2.

g.text: Every signal has even + odd parts

g.data-narration: Deterministic signals can be described by explicit mathematical formulas. Random signals follow probabilistic patterns and cannot be precisely predicted.

g.text: Random: Probabilistic behavior - no exact formula

g.data-narration: Energy signals have finite energy with zero average power. Power signals have infinite energy but finite nonzero average power.
Four fundamental elementary signals form the basis: unit step, unit impulse, unit ramp, and exponential signals.
The unit step function u(t) equals 1 for t greater than or equal to 0 and 0 for t less than 0. It represents switching on of signals.
The Dirac delta δ(t) is zero everywhere except at t equals 0 where it's infinitely high with area equal to 1.
The unit ramp r(t) equals t for t greater than or equal to 0 and 0 for t less than 0. It's the integral of the unit step.
Exponential signals have the form x(t) equals C times e to the power at. For real a, the signal grows or decays exponentially.
Time shifting moves signals horizontally. Time scaling compresses or expands. Time reversal flips the signal about the y-axis.
Key system properties include causality, linearity, time-invariance, and memory. These determine how systems process input signals.

g.text: Output depends on future or past

g.data-narration: This completes the signal concepts module. You now understand fundamental signal types, operations, and properties essential for DSP.
g.text: Key topics: Signal types, periodicity, symmetry, energy/power, elementary signals, time operations, system properties
